macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishnah on Nedarim and the Mechanism of Hefker

micro_segments.title: Setting the context: the mishnah on madir (one who makes another assur) and the solution of hefker
Rabbi Yose's position that hefker cannot work as a solution in this case
Rabbi Yochanan's explanation: hefker is like a matana and must be taken possession of first
Discussion on ownership and reshut: the concept that everything has some baalut

macro_segments.title: The Mishnah on Making a Field Hefker and the Three-Day Retraction Rule

micro_segments.title: Why someone would make their field hefker: to avoid terumot u'maasrot obligations
The rabbinical restriction: within three days one can retract hefker, even if someone has claimed it
The distinction when hefker is declared for a limited time (one day, week, month, year or seven-year cycle)
As long as no one has taken possession, retraction is possible; once someone possesses it, retraction is barred

macro_segments.title: The Attribution Dispute: Is the Mishnah's First Part Rabbanan or Rabbi Yose?

micro_segments.title: Why the rabbis permit retraction during the first three days despite hefker being a real act of dispossession
The concern about sleazeballs (rammaim) who make fields hefker only to reclaim them and avoid terumot u'maasrot
Rav's explanation: the three-day restriction limits the sleazeballs' ability to exploit the system
Ulla's position: both the first and second parts of the mishnah follow the view of the Rabbanan

macro_segments.title: Reish Lakish's Alternative Attribution and the Meaning of 'So the Idea of Hefker Will Not Be Forgotten'

micro_segments.title: Reish Lakish identifies both parts of the mishnah as reflecting Rabbi Yose's position
The Ran's interpretation: Rabbi Yose institutes a three-day period so people won't confuse the nature of hefker
The Ran's concern: if hefker is always revocable (per Rabbi Yose's true position), people will think it's a gift (matana) rather than ownerless property
The worry about mixed terumot u'maasrot: recipients will not know if the field is chayav or patur, risking improper separation

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Challenge to Reish Lakish and Rava's Resolution on the Rammaim

micro_segments.title: The Gemara questions: if Rabbi Yose's true position allows retraction until someone takes possession, why impose a three-day limit?
Rava's answer: the three-day limit is a balance between preserving the concept of hefker and preventing rammaim from exploiting the system
After three days, the field becomes fully hefker de'rabbanan so people understand it is truly ownerless
The concern that without the three-day limit, someone who reclaims after a long period would confuse people about chiyuv in terumot u'maasrot

macro_segments.title: The Baraita on Field Hefker and Exemption from Maaser: Reconciling Ulla and Reish Lakish

micro_segments.title: The baraita: one who makes his vineyard hefker and harvests in the morning is chayav in some tithes but patur from maaser
Ulla's explanation: both the baraita and the mishnah follow Rabbanan; the three-day period applies at first
Reish Lakish's challenge: if the baraita is Rabbanan, why is there exemption from maaser during the three days?
The Ran explains: since the person did not explicitly state he is reclaiming (zocheh) from the hefker, we assume he is merely retracting it
Reish Lakish's response: the entire baraita should be attributed to Rabbi Yose to avoid the difficulty

macro_segments.title: The Alternative Explanation: Hefker Before Two vs. Three Witnesses and the Effective Application of Rules

micro_segments.title: An alternative explanation distinguishes between hefker made before two people (where the three-day rule applies) and before three (where it does not)
Rabbi Yochanan and Elazar ben Anas: hefker before three is immediately fully effective
Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi: three witnesses are needed not for the Torah's requirement but for practical proof—one to take possession, two to testify
